title: Venepuncture
oneLiner: Percutaneous access to a peripheral vein for blood sampling; core skill with risk of arterial puncture, nerve injury, or infection.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Arterial puncture** — bright red, pulsatile flow, rapid swelling; ischaemic hand (pallor, pain, paralysis). *Immediate firm pressure for 10 min*, then escalate.
- **Nerve injury** — sharp electric shock sensation, distal paraesthesia/weakness (median/radial/ulnar). *Stop immediately, do not proceed*, document and refer to senior.
- **Severe haematoma with compartment syndrome** — tense, painful forearm, altered sensation, capillary refill >3 seconds. *Urgent surgical review.*
sections.Presentation:
- **Indications:** routine bloods (FBC, U&E, LFT, coagulation, cultures), IV access, therapeutic phlebotomy
- **Patient factors:** needle phobia, anticoagulation, AV fistula/graf, mastectomy side (avoid lymphedema risk), known difficult veins (thin, rolling, fragile)
- **Examination findings:** visible/palpable veins, scarring from previous procedures, skin infection at site, oedema, bruising
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- Not applicable as a procedure, but **differential for swelling post‑venepuncture:**
  - **Haematoma** – immediate, tender, firm, evolving bruise
  - **Thrombophlebitis** – delayed 12–48h, cord‑like, erythema, warmth
  - **Cellulitis** – spreading erythema, systemic signs (fever, raised CRP)
  - **Arteriovenous fistula** (rare) – palpable thrill, audible bruit
- **Splitter:** Tenderness and firmness within minutes → haematoma; delayed erythema with palpable cord → thrombophlebitis
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Assess vein patency (tourniquet, arm dependancy, tapping)
  - Use ultrasound if difficult access (if available)
  - Check bleeding history (anticoagulation, thrombocytopenia, known coagulopathy)
- **Bloods (if indicated):**
  - Coagulation screen / platelet count if bleeding risk suspected (e.g. liver disease, chemotherapy, DIC)
  - INR/APTT before deep venous puncture in anticoagulated patients
- **Imaging:** None routinely
- **Special tests:** None
- **Never‑miss test:** **Bleeding risk assessment** – patient on warfarin/DOAC/heparin? Platelet count <50? INR >3? If yes, consider venous access with smallest gauge, avoid deep veins, apply prolonged pressure. Skipping this check risks life‑threatening haematoma/compartment syndrome.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (procedure – first hour / ED):**
  - Correct order of draw: blood culture (sterile) → citrate (blue) → serum (gold/tiger) → heparin (green) → EDTA (purple) → fluoride (grey). Mix each by inversion.
  - Use smallest gauge for the sample volume (21–23G for routine, 16–18G for large volumes/cultures).
  - Apply local anaesthetic (lidocaine 1% or EMLA cream) if patient requests or is needle‑phobic – wait 2–5 min (lidocaine) or 60 min (EMLA).
  - Anchor vein 2 cm below insertion point, needle bevel up at 15–30° angle.
  - Release tourniquet once blood flows (or within 1 minute to avoid haemolysis).
  - Apply clean gauze and firm pressure for 2–5 min (longer if anticoagulated).
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Document site, gauge, any complications, whether blood sent.
  - Observe for swelling, bruising, phlebitis. If IV line left in, flush with 0.9% saline daily and change dressing if soiled.
- **Long‑term / outpatient:** N/A (single event)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Not a disease; no specific orders.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Local anaesthetic:** Lidocaine 1% (10 mg/mL) – 0.1–0.2 mL subcutaneous injection (max total dose 200 mg without adrenaline, 500 mg with adrenaline). *Check local policy* for paediatric doses.
- **Topical anaesthetic:** EMLA cream (lidocaine 2.5% + prilocaine 2.5%) – apply thick layer, occlude for at least 60 min before procedure.
- **Common alternatives:** Amethocaine gel (tetracaine 4%) – apply 30–45 min before (faster onset than EMLA).
- **Key contraindications:** Allergy to amide anaesthetics (lidocaine, prilocaine, etc.); avoid EMLA on broken skin, mucous membranes, or in infants <37 weeks.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Lidocaine half‑life prolonged in severe hepatic impairment – reduce dose; no change in renal impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Local anaesthetic overdose** (most common with multiple injections or incorrect concentration) – early CNS symptoms (perioral tingling, metallic taste, dizziness) → seizures → coma → respiratory arrest. Max doses: 3 mg/kg plain lidocaine; 7 mg/kg with adrenaline. Use smallest effective volume.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Arterial puncture (ischaemia, haematoma)
  - Nerve injury (immediate paraesthesia, later neuropraxia)
  - Haematoma / bruising (usually minor, rarely compartment syndrome)
  - Syncope (vasovagal) – place patient supine, elevate legs
  - Infection (cellulitis, bacteraemia from contaminated site)
  - Needlestick injury – immediate first aid + occupational health referral
- **Chronic:**
  - Thrombophlebitis (painful cord, erythema – treat with warm compress, NSAIDs)
  - Scarring / fibrosis (habitual repeat punctures)
  - Nerve entrapment (late manifestation of median/radial nerve injury)
sections.Prognosis: - Most venepunctures are benign; serious complications occur in <1%. Complete recovery expected from minor haematoma/vasovagal. Nerve injury may take weeks to months for full recovery; permanent deficit very rare.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- Always warn the patient: “You may feel a sharp scratch” – vasovagal syncope is common and preventable by keeping patient supine or with legs elevated.
- In difficult veins: use a vein finder device or ultrasound if available; apply warm pack for 2 min to dilate veins; lower arm below heart; ask patient to clench fist.
- Send blood cultures via dedicated sterile collection – never from an existing cannula (high contamination risk).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- Do not leave a tournique on >1 minute – causes haemolysis and stasis artefacts (pseudohyperkalaemia, false low pH).
- Do not use the inner forearm veins for large‑bore cannulas – high risk of median nerve injury. Prefer cephalic vein at wrist or dorsum of hand.
- Do not forget to label tubes at the bedside – mislabelled samples require repeat venepuncture and delay results.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Increasing pain, swelling, or redness at puncture site beyond 24h
  - Numbness, tingling, or weakness in arm/fingers
  - Fever or chills within 48h (possible infection)
- **Follow-up:** None routine; if complications, urgent GP or ED review. For nerve symptoms, refer to neurology or hand surgeon.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/IV team if:**
  - Two failed attempts (document attempt; do not attempt more than 2 to avoid patient harm)
  - Suspected arterial puncture (bright red, pulsatile, haematoma) – apply firm pressure for 10 min, then reassess; inform senior.
  - Suspected nerve injury (immediate electric shock) – stop, document, refer to anaesthetics/hand clinic.
  - Development of compartment syndrome (tense forearm, severe pain, paraesthesia, pallor, pulselessness) – **immediate** surgical review (crash call if signs of ischaemia).
- **NEWS2:** Not specifically for venepuncture; if vasovagal causes transient hypotension/bradycardia, monitor until resolved.

rawMarkdown:
## Venepuncture
**One-liner:** Percutaneous access to a peripheral vein for blood sampling; core skill with risk of arterial puncture, nerve injury, or infection.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Arterial puncture** — bright red, pulsatile flow, rapid swelling; ischaemic hand (pallor, pain, paralysis). *Immediate firm pressure for 10 min*, then escalate.
- **Nerve injury** — sharp electric shock sensation, distal paraesthesia/weakness (median/radial/ulnar). *Stop immediately, do not proceed*, document and refer to senior.
- **Severe haematoma with compartment syndrome** — tense, painful forearm, altered sensation, capillary refill >3 seconds. *Urgent surgical review.*

### Presentation
- **Indications:** routine bloods (FBC, U&E, LFT, coagulation, cultures), IV access, therapeutic phlebotomy
- **Patient factors:** needle phobia, anticoagulation, AV fistula/graf, mastectomy side (avoid lymphedema risk), known difficult veins (thin, rolling, fragile)
- **Examination findings:** visible/palpable veins, scarring from previous procedures, skin infection at site, oedema, bruising

### Differential Diagnosis
- Not applicable as a procedure, but **differential for swelling post‑venepuncture:**
  - **Haematoma** – immediate, tender, firm, evolving bruise
  - **Thrombophlebitis** – delayed 12–48h, cord‑like, erythema, warmth
  - **Cellulitis** – spreading erythema, systemic signs (fever, raised CRP)
  - **Arteriovenous fistula** (rare) – palpable thrill, audible bruit
- **Splitter:** Tenderness and firmness within minutes → haematoma; delayed erythema with palpable cord → thrombophlebitis

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Assess vein patency (tourniquet, arm dependancy, tapping)
  - Use ultrasound if difficult access (if available)
  - Check bleeding history (anticoagulation, thrombocytopenia, known coagulopathy)
- **Bloods (if indicated):**
  - Coagulation screen / platelet count if bleeding risk suspected (e.g. liver disease, chemotherapy, DIC)
  - INR/APTT before deep venous puncture in anticoagulated patients
- **Imaging:** None routinely
- **Special tests:** None
- **Never‑miss test:** **Bleeding risk assessment** – patient on warfarin/DOAC/heparin? Platelet count <50? INR >3? If yes, consider venous access with smallest gauge, avoid deep veins, apply prolonged pressure. Skipping this check risks life‑threatening haematoma/compartment syndrome.

### Management
- **Immediate (procedure – first hour / ED):**
  - Correct order of draw: blood culture (sterile) → citrate (blue) → serum (gold/tiger) → heparin (green) → EDTA (purple) → fluoride (grey). Mix each by inversion.
  - Use smallest gauge for the sample volume (21–23G for routine, 16–18G for large volumes/cultures).
  - Apply local anaesthetic (lidocaine 1% or EMLA cream) if patient requests or is needle‑phobic – wait 2–5 min (lidocaine) or 60 min (EMLA).
  - Anchor vein 2 cm below insertion point, needle bevel up at 15–30° angle.
  - Release tourniquet once blood flows (or within 1 minute to avoid haemolysis).
  - Apply clean gauze and firm pressure for 2–5 min (longer if anticoagulated).
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Document site, gauge, any complications, whether blood sent.
  - Observe for swelling, bruising, phlebitis. If IV line left in, flush with 0.9% saline daily and change dressing if soiled.
- **Long‑term / outpatient:** N/A (single event)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Not a disease; no specific orders.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Local anaesthetic:** Lidocaine 1% (10 mg/mL) – 0.1–0.2 mL subcutaneous injection (max total dose 200 mg without adrenaline, 500 mg with adrenaline). *Check local policy* for paediatric doses.
- **Topical anaesthetic:** EMLA cream (lidocaine 2.5% + prilocaine 2.5%) – apply thick layer, occlude for at least 60 min before procedure.
- **Common alternatives:** Amethocaine gel (tetracaine 4%) – apply 30–45 min before (faster onset than EMLA).
- **Key contraindications:** Allergy to amide anaesthetics (lidocaine, prilocaine, etc.); avoid EMLA on broken skin, mucous membranes, or in infants <37 weeks.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Lidocaine half‑life prolonged in severe hepatic impairment – reduce dose; no change in renal impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Local anaesthetic overdose** (most common with multiple injections or incorrect concentration) – early CNS symptoms (perioral tingling, metallic taste, dizziness) → seizures → coma → respiratory arrest. Max doses: 3 mg/kg plain lidocaine; 7 mg/kg with adrenaline. Use smallest effective volume.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Arterial puncture (ischaemia, haematoma)
  - Nerve injury (immediate paraesthesia, later neuropraxia)
  - Haematoma / bruising (usually minor, rarely compartment syndrome)
  - Syncope (vasovagal) – place patient supine, elevate legs
  - Infection (cellulitis, bacteraemia from contaminated site)
  - Needlestick injury – immediate first aid + occupational health referral
- **Chronic:**
  - Thrombophlebitis (painful cord, erythema – treat with warm compress, NSAIDs)
  - Scarring / fibrosis (habitual repeat punctures)
  - Nerve entrapment (late manifestation of median/radial nerve injury)

### Prognosis
- Most venepunctures are benign; serious complications occur in <1%. Complete recovery expected from minor haematoma/vasovagal. Nerve injury may take weeks to months for full recovery; permanent deficit very rare.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- Always warn the patient: “You may feel a sharp scratch” – vasovagal syncope is common and preventable by keeping patient supine or with legs elevated.
- In difficult veins: use a vein finder device or ultrasound if available; apply warm pack for 2 min to dilate veins; lower arm below heart; ask patient to clench fist.
- Send blood cultures via dedicated sterile collection – never from an existing cannula (high contamination risk).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- Do not leave a tournique on >1 minute – causes haemolysis and stasis artefacts (pseudohyperkalaemia, false low pH).
- Do not use the inner forearm veins for large‑bore cannulas – high risk of median nerve injury. Prefer cephalic vein at wrist or dorsum of hand.
- Do not forget to label tubes at the bedside – mislabelled samples require repeat venepuncture and delay results.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Increasing pain, swelling, or redness at puncture site beyond 24h
  - Numbness, tingling, or weakness in arm/fingers
  - Fever or chills within 48h (possible infection)
- **Follow-up:** None routine; if complications, urgent GP or ED review. For nerve symptoms, refer to neurology or hand surgeon.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/IV team if:**
  - Two failed attempts (document attempt; do not attempt more than 2 to avoid patient harm)
  - Suspected arterial puncture (bright red, pulsatile, haematoma) – apply firm pressure for 10 min, then reassess; inform senior.
  - Suspected nerve injury (immediate electric shock) – stop, document, refer to anaesthetics/hand clinic.
  - Development of compartment syndrome (tense forearm, severe pain, paraesthesia, pallor, pulselessness) – **immediate** surgical review (crash call if signs of ischaemia).
- **NEWS2:** Not specifically for venepuncture; if vasovagal causes transient hypotension/bradycardia, monitor until resolved.
